Every claim in the summary must be grounded in the source text. Remove any meta-commentary, interpretive framing, or information not present in the source. Preserve entity density and the ~60 word length constraint.\n</objective>"} {:role "user", :content "<source_text>\nCRISPR-Cas9, developed as a gene-editing tool by Jennifer Doudna at UC Berkeley and Emmanuelle Charpentier at the Max Planck Institute, earned them the 2020 Nobel Prize in Chemistry. The system uses a guide RNA to direct the Cas9 enzyme to a specific DNA sequence, where it creates a double-strand break. The cell's repair machinery then either disables the gene (via non-homologous end joining) or inserts a new sequence (via homology-directed repair). Since 2013, CRISPR has been applied to human diseases including sickle cell disease, beta-thalassemia, and certain cancers. In December 2023, the FDA approved Casgevy (exagamglogene autotemcel), developed by Vertex Pharmaceuticals and CRISPR Therapeutics, as the first CRISPR-based therapy for sickle cell disease. Despite its precision, off-target effects remain a concern — the enzyme occasionally cuts DNA at unintended sites, potentially causing harmful mutations.\n</source_text>\n\n<summary_to_verify>\nCRISPR-Cas9, developed by Jennifer Doudna at UC Berkeley and Emmanuelle Charpentier at the Max Planck Institute, won the 2020 Nobel Prize in Chemistry. It uses guide RNA and the Cas9 enzyme to edit DNA. In 2023, the FDA approved Casgevy by Vertex Pharmaceuticals and CRISPR Therapeutics for sickle cell disease. Off-target effects, including unintended DNA cuts, remain a concern.\n</summary_to_verify>"} {:role "user", :content "Answer in JSON using this schema:\n{\n  // The refined, faithful summary (required)\n  summary: string,\n}"}]}}

Preserve entity density and the ~60 word length constraint.\n</objective>"} {:role "user", :content "<source_text>\nCRISPR-Cas9, developed as a gene-editing tool by Jennifer Doudna at UC Berkeley and Emmanuelle Charpentier at the Max Planck Institute, earned them the 2020 Nobel Prize in Chemistry. The system uses a guide RNA to direct the Cas9 enzyme to a specific DNA sequence, where it creates a double-strand break. The cell's repair machinery then either disables the gene (via non-homologous end joining) or inserts a new sequence (via homology-directed repair). Since 2013, CRISPR has been applied to human diseases including sickle cell disease, beta-thalassemia, and certain cancers. In December 2023, the FDA approved Casgevy (exagamglogene autotemcel), developed by Vertex Pharmaceuticals and CRISPR Therapeutics, as the first CRISPR-based therapy for sickle cell disease. Despite its precision, off-target effects remain a concern — the enzyme occasionally cuts DNA at unintended sites, potentially causing harmful mutations.\n</source_text>\n\n<summary_to_verify>\nCRISPR-Cas9, created by Jennifer Doudna at UC Berkeley and Emmanuelle Charpentier at the Max Planck Institute, earned the 2020 Nobel Prize in Chemistry. Despite its precision, off-target effects remain a concern.\n</summary_to_verify>"} {:role "user", :content "Answer in JSON using this schema:\n{\n  // The refined, faithful summary (required)\n  summary: string,\n}"}]}}
